# Plan: speckit-workflow-resilience
<!-- Generated by /speckit.plan; REVISION 3 — rewritten after /speckit.review returned unanimous REVISE_PLAN -->
<!-- Date: 2026-07-16 -->

## Research Notes

Truth Map in `research.md`. Revision 2 exists because three independent reviewers rejected revision 1.
Every finding below was **verified by execution**, not by agreement:

1. **Revision 1's fix introduced the bug it exists to kill.** `:358` reads `if (gate && !gate.passed)`.
   Today a dying gate *throws* (`:342` is a bare `await agent(...)`) and kills the run loudly. Routing
   it through `agentTyped` turns the throw into `null`, which falls straight through. Measured: both
   phase gates dead, run returned `{"completed":2,"total":2}`. That is `:320`'s silent drop
   reintroduced one function away, by the fix for `:320`.
2. **Revision 1's SC-005 test could not fail.** Its stub did `return canned(o.label)` without `await`,
   so `finally { inFlight-- }` ran before the returned promise settled. `peak` read **1 forever** — delete
   the semaphore entirely and it stayed green. Spike 3's stub *did* await; the sketch dropped it in
   transcription. A test that cannot fail, inside the criterion written to prevent tests that cannot
   fail, in a repo whose constitution names that failure as principle 3.
3. **`assert.ok(peak <= 4)` is a ceiling with no floor** — it passes *more easily the more broken the
   file is*. Break the loader ⇒ peak 1 ⇒ green. Any concurrency assertion needs a floor and a shape
   check. (Carried to B.)
4. **The version bump breaks CI as planned.** `smoke.sh:69-94` checks six declarations; revision 1
   listed three. `README.md` (footer **and** title) and `.claude/CLAUDE.md` (title) are checked as
   `major.minor` against `plugin.json`.
5. **FR-016 was unreachable.** Fixing FR-004 halts the phase, and the halt object has no `total`.
   Two reviewers found this independently. → CLR-003.

Load-bearing and unchanged from revision 1: **`:256` already treats a null implementer as
`accepted:false`.** The whole fix routes throws into a contract that already exists.

## Affected Files

| File | Change Type | Description |
|------|------------|-------------|
| `workflows/speckit-workflow.js` | modify | `agentTyped` (FR-001..003); kill the silent drop (FR-004); null gate halts (FR-005); halt ledger (FR-006, FR-007) |
| `tests/workflow.test.js` | create | `node --test` harness — strict mode, yielding stubs, label-multiset assertions (FR-008, FR-009) |
| `tests/smoke.sh` | modify | Five shape guards (1, 2, 5, 6 carried from review, plus guard 7 for FR-005) (FR-011), each mutation-tested (SC-010) |
| `.github/workflows/smoke.yml` | modify | Add `node --test`; no `setup-node` (FR-010, SC-011). **Not `node --test tests/`** — passing the directory makes node load it as a module and fail with `Cannot find module .../tests`. Bare `node --test` discovers `*.test.js` from the root. Corrected during implementation after the first real run; four review rounds missed it |
| `CHANGELOG.md` | modify | 5.1.0 entry |
| `.claude-plugin/plugin.json` | modify | version → 5.1.0 |
| `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json` | modify | `metadata.version` **and** `plugins[0].version` → 5.1.0 |
| `README.md` | modify | **Footer `Framework Version**: 5.1.0` AND title `v5.1`** — missed in revision 1; smoke gates on both |
| `.claude/CLAUDE.md` | modify | **Title `v5.1`** — missed in revision 1; smoke checks it as `major.minor` |

> `.claude/CLAUDE.md` is not plugin payload (constitution principle 1) yet smoke gates a release on its
> title. That is intentional — it is this repo's own config and it advertises the version — but it is
> worth knowing the two facts coexist.

## Data Model

No persistent data model. **Two** shapes change — the halt return, and (declared, not accidental) the
clean-completion return:

```
Halt := { halted: true, haltedAt: string, reason: string,
          total: number,           // every pending task in the run
          accepted: string[],      // ids verified complete
          rejected: [{id, description, reason}],
          notAttempted: string[] } // pending tasks in phases that never ran

Clean := { featureDir, completed, total, tasks, report,   // unchanged from v5.0.0
           accepted, rejected, notAttempted }             // NEW — ledger() is shared with the halt sites
```

**Back-compat declaration.** A successful run is otherwise byte-identical to v5.0.0: `agentTyped`
returns `await agent(...)` unchanged on first success; Sketch 2's `r || {...}` is identical to
`.filter(Boolean)` when every `r` is truthy; Sketch 3's `!gate || !gate.passed` is identical when the
gate is truthy. FR-007's `total` is provably equal to today's `results.length` (`:418`) on a clean run,
because `results` accumulates exactly `ph.tasks.filter(t => !t.done)` per phase (`:286`, `:327`) and
empty phases `continue` at `:287`. The **one** exception is additive: sharing `ledger()` with the final
return adds `accepted`/`rejected`/`notAttempted` to the success shape. Additive and low-risk, but it is
a shape change on the success path and it is declared here rather than discovered later.

## API Contracts

No external API. No `args` change in A — `maxConcurrency`, `sequential`, `repos`, and `projectRoot` all
belong to B and C. **A adds no new argument surface**, which is why A carries none of the argument
validation defects the adversary measured.

## The null audit — all six `agent(` sites

> **This table covers exactly one class: `null` returns from `agent()`.** It is not an audit of every
> place this file treats *"we do not know"* as *"everything is fine"* — that class is larger, and two
> further instances were found while reviewing A (`testCommand: ""`, and `redConfirmed`/`greenConfirmed`
> being collected and never read). Both are pre-existing, untouched by A, and filed rather than fixed
> here; see `research.md` §Open Questions. The narrow title is deliberate: reading a narrow audit as a
> broad one is precisely the over-read that produced revisions 1 and 2.

FR-003 routes every call through `agentTyped`, which makes `null` reachable at **every** site. Revision 1
audited none of them and claimed principle 5 passed. Revision 2 audited one (`:358`) and claimed victory.
The audit is a deliverable, not a requirement — nothing can falsify a table's completeness, and an FR
that nothing can falsify is principle 3's own defect wearing a different hat. SC-014's guard is a
**tripwire** that forces re-audit when a seventh site appears; it does not verify this table. Enumerated
by `grep -nP '(?<![A-Za-z0-9_$.])agent\s*\(' workflows/speckit-workflow.js`:

| Site | Label | Null handled today? | Disposition in A |
|------|-------|---------------------|------------------|
| `:205` | `load-artifacts` | ✅ `:240` — `if (!ctx \|\| !ctx.phases…)` returns an error | unchanged |
| `:251` | `impl:<id>` | ✅ `:256` — `accepted:false, reason:'implementer died or was skipped'` | unchanged; this is the contract everything else routes into |
| `:262` | `verify:<id>:<lens>` | ⚠️ `.filter(Boolean)` at `:269` — 2-of-3 nulls ⇒ accepted on one lens | **deferred, with an issue.** `agentTyped` cannot *raise* the null rate here: `parallel` already converts a verifier throw to null before `:269` sees it, so retry can only *reduce* it. A does not make this worse — but it must not be lost either |
| `:342` | `gate:<phase>` | ❌ `:358` `if (gate && !gate.passed)` — **null silently passes** | **FR-005.** The regression A's own fix introduces |
| `:376` | `update-tasks-md` | ❌ result **discarded** — `await agent(...)`, unassigned, no schema | **FR-012.** Null ⇒ no checkbox ticked, run still reports `N/N` clean |
| `:386` | `completion-report` | ⚠️ lands as `report: null` in the return — visible, not silent | **FR-012.** Acknowledge explicitly; a null report is at least legible to the operator |

Two honest notes. `:376` and `:386` nulls **partly pre-exist** — a terminal API error already returns
null at both today — so A widens these paths rather than creating them; only `:342` is a regression A
introduces outright. And `:376` has no schema, so whether it can *throw* at all is unclear: the
documented throw is StructuredOutput-specific. That uncertainty is a reason to handle the null, not to
assume it away.

## Implementation Approach

Four changes, each independently verifiable.

1. **`agentTyped` — the choke point (FR-001..003).** Owns exactly two concerns in A: retry-on-throw,
   and never-retry-null. The semaphore and throttle counter that made revision 1's version a
   four-concern object are B's. Routing every call through one function is what lets B add the
   semaphore in one place later, and what FR-003's smoke guard enforces.
2. **Kill the silent drop (FR-004).** Replace `.filter(Boolean)` at `:320` with an index-mapped
   fallback.
3. **Null gate halts (FR-005).** `if (!gate || !gate.passed)`. This is not optional cleanup — it is the
   regression change 1 introduces. Landing 1 without 3 is strictly worse than landing neither.
4. **Halt ledger (FR-006, FR-007).**

**Rejected simplification**: extracting `withRetry(agent)` as a separate composable. `review-scope`
argued the choke point fuses concerns; in A it holds only two, and the harness `eval`s one file with no
module system, so extraction buys closures in the same file rather than separation. Revisit in B, where
the semaphore genuinely is a distinct policy.

## Code Sketches (Mental Alignment)

### Sketch 1: `agentTyped` — retry a throw, never retry a null
**File:** `workflows/speckit-workflow.js` (new, above `implementAndVerify:250`)
**Intent:** one place every agent call passes through.

```js
// A THROW means the agent finished without calling StructuredOutput — cheap to re-ask, so retry.
// A NULL means the harness ALREADY exhausted its own backoff against a terminal API error. Retrying
// that pours load onto an API that is already refusing us. Only the throw is retried. (FR-001, FR-002)
async function agentTyped(prompt, opts, tries = 2) {
  for (let i = 0; ; i++) {
    try {
      return await agent(prompt, opts)
    } catch (e) {
      // `e?.message` and `opts?.label`, NOT `e.message` / `opts.label`. A thrown null or undefined makes
      // the error handler ITSELF throw a TypeError — measured: `throw null` => "Cannot read properties of
      // null (reading 'message')" propagates out and KILLS THE SEQUENTIAL RUN, on exactly the scenario
      // US1 exists to prevent. The [P] path survives only because Sketch 2 catches it via parallel — so
      // the failure inverts this feature's own thesis: here sequential dies and parallel is protected.
      // The harness's throw TYPE is undocumented, so reachability is unknown; the cost of not caring is
      // two characters. (FR-001)
      const why = e?.message ?? String(e)
      if (i >= tries) { log(`${opts?.label}: gave up after ${tries + 1} attempts — ${why}`); return null }
      log(`${opts?.label}: retry ${i + 1}/${tries} — ${why}`)
    }
  }
}
```
**Why this shape:** returning `null` on exhaustion routes a dead agent into the contract `:256` already
honours (`accepted:false`) rather than inventing one. Two concerns, not four — the semaphore is B's.
`tries` is bounded at 3 attempts (FR-001, SC-015): unbounded retry against a hot API is the failure the
field report describes, and nothing else pins it.

### Sketch 2: kill the silent drop
**File:** `workflows/speckit-workflow.js` (modify `:320`)
**Intent:** a crashed `[P]` implementer becomes a visible rejection, not an absence.

```js
// Do NOT .filter(Boolean) here. parallel() converts a thrown implementer to null, and filtering it
// ERASES the task: it lands in neither accepted nor rejected, the halt at :329 cannot see it, and the
// run returns 1/1 — a PERFECT SCORE for a phase where the task never ran. Measured; research.md Spike 2.
const done = await parallel(b.tasks.map(t => () => implementAndVerify(t)))
done.forEach((r, i) =>
  phaseResults.push(r || { task: b.tasks[i], accepted: false, reason: 'implementer crashed (unhandled error)' }))
```
**Why this shape:** index-mapped, so the null still knows which task it was. Belt-and-braces with
Sketch 1 (which should stop throws escaping at all) because `parallel`'s throw→null contract is the
harness's, not ours — if it ever changes, this line still fails loudly instead of quietly.

### Sketch 3: a dead gate halts — it does not pass
**File:** `workflows/speckit-workflow.js` (modify `:358`)
**Intent:** absence of confirmation is not confirmation.

```js
// `gate && !gate.passed` let a NULL gate through: agentTyped returns null when the gate agent dies, so
// the phase silently skipped its gate and the next phase built on ungated work. Measured: both gates
// dead => {"completed":2,"total":2}. Before Sketch 1 this path was unreachable-by-null because :342
// THREW and killed the run; Sketch 1 is what makes null possible here, so this line ships WITH it.
// Note the asymmetry with :353 — a project that CONFIGURES no gate is an absence of a gate and reports
// passed:true. A gate agent that DIED is not that, and must never be mistaken for it. (FR-005)
if (!gate || !gate.passed) {
  return { halted: true, haltedAt: ph.name,
    reason: gate ? `Phase quality gate failed: ${gate.summary}`
                 : 'Phase gate agent died — the suite was never confirmed. Refusing to build the next phase on it.',
    failures: gate?.failures || [],   // :364 carried this. Dropping it loses the failing-test list at
                                      // exactly the moment the gate fails. Kept deliberately. (SC-016)
    ...ledger(results, ctx) }
}
```
**Why this shape:** the two reasons are distinct strings because "the suite failed" and "we never learned
whether the suite passed" are different operator problems — and SC-004 asserts on *which* string, so a
fixture reaching the right answer for the wrong reason cannot pass.

**The asymmetry with `:353` is safe, and this was tested rather than assumed.** A gateless project
honouring `:353` returns an **object** (`{passed:true, summary:"no gate configured"}`), so `!gate` is
false and it does not halt — verified: `{"completed":2,"total":2}, halted? false`. `null` is reachable
only via agent death or retry exhaustion. Those are structurally different values, so `!gate || !gate.passed`
cannot confuse "no gate configured" with "the gate died".

**Known cost, priced not hidden**: a gate that throws is now retried 3×, so a dying gate re-runs the full
suite three times — on FxCube's 728-test/~12-min suite that is ~36 minutes. A introduces this; bounding
it belongs to B (#29), which owns suite-run amplitude.

### Sketch 4: the halt ledger
**File:** `workflows/speckit-workflow.js` (new helper; used by both halt sites and the final return)
**Intent:** every exit path reports what actually happened.

```js
// Both halt sites (:330, :358) returned no `total`, so "how much got done?" was unanswerable exactly
// when it mattered most. One helper, three call sites. (FR-006, FR-007)
//
// Identity is POSITIONAL, never task.id. tasks.md is parsed by an LLM (:205-236) and nothing validates
// that ids are unique across phases — so keying on id lets a duplicate T001 in phase 3 count as
// "attempted" because phase 1's T001 ran, and notAttempted UNDER-REPORTS. The ledger would lie, inside
// the helper written to stop the ledger lying. This file already refuses to trust the model-written [P]
// marker and re-derives safety from file lists (:293-297); ids earn exactly the same distrust. (FR-013)
// A `function` declaration, NOT `const keyOf = ...`. The stamp (below) runs at :241, ~11 lines ABOVE
// this; a const is in its temporal dead zone until its declaration executes, so the const arrow throws
// `ReferenceError: Cannot access 'keyOf' before initialization` on EVERY driven scenario — including the
// clean run. Measured. A function declaration hoists and the problem evaporates. This is the THIRD
// ordering defect in four revisions (1→3, 3→4, stamp→keyOf); see §Declaration order.
function keyOf(pi, ti) { return `${pi}:${ti}` }

const ledger = (results, ctx) => {
  const attempted = new Set(results.map(r => r.task._key))   // reads the stamp; never recomputes it
  // Read `t._key` — do NOT recompute it here. Deriving it twice means the two derivations agree only
  // while BOTH index the unfiltered p.tasks. Transpose either to filter-then-map and T002 reindexes
  // from 0:1 to 0:0 and reappears in notAttempted despite having run — the exact drift _key exists to
  // prevent. One source of truth. (FR-013)
  const pending = ctx.phases.flatMap(p => p.tasks).filter(t => !t.done)
  return {
    total: pending.length,
    accepted: results.filter(r => r.accepted).map(r => r.task.id),
    rejected: results.filter(r => !r.accepted).map(r => ({ id: r.task.id, description: r.task.description, reason: r.reason })),
    notAttempted: pending.filter(t => !attempted.has(t._key)).map(t => t.id),
  }
}
```
**Why this shape:** `notAttempted` is derived from the task graph rather than tracked, so it cannot drift
from what actually ran — but the derivation needs an identity the model cannot forge, which `id` is not.
`accepted`/`rejected` still *display* `id` because that is what the operator reads in `tasks.md`; only
the set arithmetic uses `_key`.

**The stamp goes AFTER the `:240` guard, not after `:238`.** Revision 3 said "immediately after `:238`",
which places it before `if (!ctx || !ctx.phases …)` — so a dead loader throws
`TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'phases')` instead of returning its clean
"No tasks.md found" error, **breaking the one null path the audit table marks ✅ handled**. Measured.

```js
// immediately AFTER the :240 guard, once ctx is known non-null:
ctx.phases.forEach((p, pi) => p.tasks.forEach((t, ti) => { t._key = keyOf(pi, ti) }))
```

## Declaration order — stated once, because we keep finding it one line at a time

Three revisions produced three ordering defects: Sketch 1 without Sketch 3 (silent gate pass), Sketch 3
without Sketch 4 (`ReferenceError: ledger is not defined`), and the stamp without `keyOf` (`ReferenceError:
Cannot access 'keyOf' before initialization`). Each was found by a reviewer hand-simulating the
interpreter. Rather than wait for the fourth, the file's required order, top to bottom:

1. `function keyOf(...)` — hoisted, so placement is advisory; declared here for readers.
2. `agentTyped` — above its first use (the loader call at `:205`).
3. loader `agent()` call → **`:240` null guard** → **`_key` stamp**. In that order, never reordered.
4. `ledger` — above `implementAndVerify:250`, used by all three exits.
5. `implementAndVerify`, the phase loop, the gate check, the Report phase.

**This list is documentation, not enforcement — a reader can violate it and nothing goes red.** The real
enforcement is `node --test`, which throws on every one of these instantly. That is the whole argument
for writing the code rather than reviewing more sketches.

### Sketch 6: a run whose tasks.md was never written must not report clean
**File:** `workflows/speckit-workflow.js` (modify `:376`)
**Intent:** FR-012 — the ❌ row the audit table diagnosed and revision 3 then implemented with nothing.

```js
// :376 DISCARDED its result. A null means no checkbox was ticked — and the run still reported
// {"completed":3,"total":3} (measured). A green run whose only persisted artifact never happened, and
// whose next invocation re-implements every task.
//
// `=== null`, NOT `!ticked`: :376 has no schema, so its return type is unspecified and a legitimate
// empty-string return would false-positive. agentTyped returns null and only null on failure.
//
// The reason must distinguish "the work failed" from "the bookkeeping failed" — everything IS done and
// adversarially verified, only the write failed. An operator who reads this as a normal halt re-runs
// work that is already on disk. (FR-012, SC-012)
const ticked = await agentTyped(tasksMdPrompt(accepted, ctx), { label: 'update-tasks-md', phase: 'Report' })
if (ticked === null) {
  return { halted: true, haltedAt: 'Report',
    reason: 'All tasks were implemented and verified, but the tasks.md update agent died — the checkboxes ' +
            'were NOT written. The work is on disk and complete; do not re-run the implementation. Tick the ' +
            'listed task ids by hand, or re-run this step alone.',
    ...ledger(results, ctx) }
}
```
**Why this shape:** halting is FR-005's principle applied to the site FR-005's principle was written from —
absence of confirmation is not confirmation. The alternative (a `tasksUpdated:false` field) would be a
value nobody reads, which is precisely #32's defect in this same file: evidence collected and discarded.

### Sketch 5: the harness — strict, yielding, and floored
**File:** `tests/workflow.test.js` (new)
**Intent:** drive the shipped file under the semantics it ships with; assert at the tool-call level.

```js
const raw = fs.readFileSync('workflows/speckit-workflow.js', 'utf8')
const src = raw.replace('export const meta', 'const meta')
assert.notStrictEqual(src, raw, 'meta rewrite matched nothing — the harness would test a stale shape')  // SC-006
// 'use strict' FIRST: a new Function body is SLOPPY, but the shipped file is an ES module and always
// strict. Without this the harness runs different semantics from production — an undeclared assignment
// (an ordinary typo) passes green here and throws ReferenceError on the first real run. (FR-008, SC-007)
const run = new Function('agent', 'parallel', 'phase', 'log', 'args',
  `'use strict'; return (async () => { ${src}\n })()`)

// The stub MUST await, and MUST mirror parallel()'s documented throw->null contract. Revision 1's stub
// did `return canned(...)` un-awaited, so `finally` fired before the promise settled and every
// concurrency reading was 1 — an assertion that could not fail.
const parallel = async ts => Promise.all(ts.map(async t => { try { return await t() } catch { return null } }))
const spawned = []
const agent = async (p, o) => { spawned.push(o.label); await new Promise(r => setImmediate(r)); return canned(o.label) }

// Assert the exact label multiset, not just an outcome: a workflow that breaks early and spawns
// nothing must not be able to pass by doing less. (SC-008)
assert.deepStrictEqual(spawned.sort(), EXPECTED_LABELS.sort())
```
**Why this shape:** asserts on **which agents were spawned**, never on data an agent returned —
constitution principle 3, the lesson that once went green against a completely broken plugin.

> **Known limits, stated rather than papered over.** (a) The loader is stubbed, so nothing here tests
> that a real model parses `tasks.md` correctly — only that the script consumes the parsed shape.
> (b) The stubs are our imitation of the real harness; a comment cannot detect drift. Defence: run every
> resilience test **twice**, against both plausible `parallel()` contracts — throw→null *and*
> propagate — and assert an explicit rejection under both. Then "which contract is real?" stops
> mattering. Only a `SMOKE_LIVE` tier-2 probe can confirm the real one, and it cannot run in CI.

## Test Fixture Design

The spec says *what must be true*; this says *how the fixture makes each mutation actually fire*. Every
requirement below was earned — each is a case where a reviewer proved the mutation would not fire without
it. They are not style notes.

| For | The fixture must | Because |
|-----|------------------|---------|
| SC-001 | Throw on **every** attempt, and include a **non-Error** throw (`throw null`) | Throw once and `agentTyped` retries, succeeds, and the phase never halts — so "halts the phase" is untested while "the run does not throw" passes. The non-Error case is measured to kill the run via `e.message` in the catch (Spike 6) |
| SC-002 | Apply the **pair** mutation: restore `.filter(Boolean)` **and** remove the try/catch | Restoring the filter alone is a **proven no-op**: with Sketch 1 present, `agentTyped` catches the throw, `:256` converts the null to a truthy `{accepted:false}`, so `implementAndVerify` never returns null and the filter filters nothing. Executed: `MUTATED -> rejected:['T001'], total:2` — identical to fixed. The pair yields `rejected:[], total:1` — Spike 2's `1/1` |
| SC-003, SC-015 | Count invocations **on the stub**, **per label** | The retry loop lives *inside* `agentTyped`, so a retry-on-null mutation is invisible from outside it. A global count is muddied by the other agents in the fixture |
| SC-004 | Three **positive floors**: (a) assert `gate:P1` **was** spawned; (b) assert phase 1's exact label multiset; (c) assert `haltedAt` **and** that `reason` is the gate-*died* string — not the gate-*failed* string, not a task-rejection reason | The negative alone ("no phase-2 `impl:` label") is vacuous — it holds whenever phase 2 does not run for *any* reason. Executed: with a phase-1 task failing verification, the `:329` halt fires **before** the gate spawns, so **the mutation goes GREEN with the gate logic entirely deleted**. Sketch 3's two distinct reason strings exist so (c) can discriminate |
| SC-005 | Drive **all three** exits: the `:330` task-rejection halt, the `:358` gate halt, and the clean return | `ledger()` has three call sites; driving one leaves two untested |
| SC-007 | Use a single `makeRun(src)` for **both** the real load and the strict fixture | If the test hand-writes its own wrapper, it proves the *test's* wrapper is strict while the real load silently loses `'use strict'` and stays green. The mutation must have exactly one site to delete from |
| SC-017 | Contain **≥2 phases AND a `done:true` task** | With one phase `notAttempted` is `[]`, and a mutation returning `[]` stays green. Without a `done:true` task, `!t.done` is undiscriminating |
| all resilience tests | Run **twice** — against a throw→null `parallel` and a propagating one | See the inversion note below: this is vacuous in A *except* against SC-002's pair mutation, which is exactly when it earns its keep |
| all | Use `[...spawned].sort()`, never `spawned.sort()` | The in-place sort destroys spawn order for any later order-sensitive assertion. Cosmetic in A; not once B needs ordering |

## The guard/test inversion — state it, do not let it be discovered

**For FR-004, the smoke guards are the PRIMARY defence, not the backstop.** SC-002's behaviour test
cannot fail on its own (the mutation is a proven no-op — Sketch 1 stops any null reaching `.filter`), so
guards 5 and 6 are the only thing standing between `:320` and a silent regression. That inverts the usual
"grep is the backstop, behaviour is the proof" relationship, and it is exactly the fact that gets lost in
a handoff. The dual-contract discipline (running each resilience test against both `parallel()` contracts)
is likewise **vacuous in A as specified** — with Sketch 1 present the thunk never rejects, so both stubs
behave identically. It earns its keep only against SC-002's *pair* mutation. Keep it; say why.

Guards 1, 2, 5, 6 carry over from review; **guards 3 and 4 are absent from A by design** — they are the
lens/gate full-suite pair, which belongs to B (#29) along with the narrowing they check. The numbering is
kept non-contiguous on purpose so the gap is visible rather than looking like an oversight.
`grep -nP '(?<![A-Za-z0-9_$.])agent\s*\('` was verified against
the real file — it returns exactly the six raw calls and does **not** match `agentTyped(`. Two known
weaknesses, both to be documented rather than fixed by weakening the match: it must count **occurrences**
(`grep -o | wc -l`), since `grep -c` counts lines and two calls on one line would pass; and the file is
~60% prompt prose, so a comment reading `` `agent(` `` trips it — the correct response is to reword the
prose, never to loosen the guard. **Guard 7 is new and is the highest-value guard in A:**

```bash
# 2. Count OCCURRENCES, not lines. `grep -c` counts LINES: two raw calls smuggled onto ONE line read as
#    1 and the bypass ships — verified (`grep -c` => 1 passes; `grep -o | wc -l` => 2 catches). Revision 3
#    stated this rule in prose and then shipped a `grep -c` guard that violated it. (FR-003, SC-014)
#
#    This pipe is SAFE and must NOT be "fixed" into capture-then-match. The header bans `producer | grep -q`,
#    where grep exits on first match and SIGPIPEs the producer. `wc -l` reads to EOF and never exits early:
#    no SIGPIPE, no race. Here grep is the PRODUCER, not the matcher.
raw_n="$(grep -oP '(?<![A-Za-z0-9_$.])agent\s*\(' "$WF" | wc -l || true)"
if [ "$raw_n" -eq 1 ]; then
  ok "exactly one raw agent( occurrence — every other call routes through agentTyped (FR-003, SC-014)"
else
  bad "$raw_n raw agent( occurrences, expected exactly 1 (the one inside agentTyped) — the rest bypass the retry choke point"
  sed 's|^|       |' <<<"$(grep -nP '(?<![A-Za-z0-9_$.])agent\s*\(' "$WF" || true)" | head -6
fi

# 7. A dead gate must HALT, not fall through. Sketch 1 is what makes null possible here: today :342 is a
#    bare `await agent(...)` that THROWS and kills the run loudly, so this path is unreachable-by-null.
#    Routing the gate through agentTyped converts that throw to null, which `gate && !gate.passed` lets
#    straight through. Measured: both gates dead => {"completed":2,"total":2}. (FR-005)
#
#    Ceiling AND floor. The negative alone passes if the gate block is DELETED; the positive alone passes
#    if a second, wrong check is added beside the right one. The PAIR is the invariant.
#
#    BOTH halves are anchored to `^[[:space:]]*if` — this is load-bearing, not tidiness. Unanchored:
#      * FALSE GREEN: comment OUT the fix (`// if (!gate || !gate.passed) {`) and the negative finds no
#        bug while the positive still matches the commented line. Both green, gate check gone.
#      * FALSE ALARM: this repo's convention REQUIRES a comment stating the trap, and the natural wording
#        quotes the code — `// if (gate && !gate.passed) let a NULL gate through` — reddening a CORRECT
#        file, which is how a guard gets deleted. Anchoring fixes both: 4/4 on the fixtures.
#
#    KNOWN LIABILITY, documented rather than papered over: the floor pins one spelling and rejects six
#    legitimate ones — line-wrapped, `if ((!gate) || (!gate.passed))`, `if (gate === null || ...)`,
#    `if (!gate?.passed)` (the idiomatic simplification a reviewer would suggest), reordered operands,
#    `if(` with no space. If a formatter or a cleanup trips it, REWORD THE CODE OR WIDEN THE MATCH
#    DELIBERATELY — never delete the guard. The pair's logic is right; its regex is the liability.
bad_gate="$(grep -nE '^[[:space:]]*if \(gate[[:space:]]*&&[[:space:]]*!gate\.passed\)' "$WF" || true)"
if [ -n "$bad_gate" ]; then
  bad "the phase gate falls through on a NULL gate — a dead gate agent would pass the phase (FR-005)"
  sed 's|^|       |' <<<"$bad_gate"
else
  ok "no fall-through on a null gate"
fi

good_gate="$(grep -nE '^[[:space:]]*if \(!gate[[:space:]]*\|\|[[:space:]]*!gate\.passed\)' "$WF" || true)"
if [ -n "$good_gate" ]; then
  ok "a dead gate halts the phase — absence of confirmation is not confirmation (FR-005)"
else
  bad "the null-gate halt is GONE — grep found neither the bug nor the fix, so the negative half is passing on nothing (FR-005)"
fi
```

## Constitution Compliance

- [x] **1 — Payload never under `.claude/`**: nothing moves. `tests/` is not a declared component
      (`plugin.json:24-36`); verified against `smoke.sh:107`, which scans `.claude/{commands,agents,skills,hooks,workflows}`.
- [x] **2 — Commands namespaced**: no new commands.
- [x] **3 — Mutation-tested, tool-call assertions**: every SC now carries an explicit mutation that must
      go red (see the spec's SC table). Revision 1 failed this on its own terms — SC-005's test could not
      fail. The five smoke guards are each mutation-tested per SC-010.
- [x] **4 — Zero-install**: **RATIFIED by the repo owner (joaoariedi), 2026-07-16.** The circularity below
      is real and was surfaced rather than papered over; a human with authority over the constitution has
      now signed it. The box is checked by ratification, not by self-assessment. Original disclosure:
      `constitution.md:37-38` pre-authorises `node --test` verbatim — and I wrote that carve-out earlier
      in this same session, then cited it as proof that my own node dependency complies. `.specify/` is
      untracked, so there is no git history to show the carve-out predates the feature. The reasoning
      stands on its merits (no `package.json`, no lockfile, no install step, node already required to run
      the tests at all) but the *authority* is circular. **Flagged for human ratification.**
- [x] **5 — Fail loudly, no ignorable sentinel**: FR-005 is the whole point. Revision 1 claimed this
      principle passed while *introducing* a violation at `:358`. Revision 2 fixes it and treats the
      claim as a thing to verify, not assert. Remaining known null-swallow: `:269` filters null verdicts,
      so 2-of-3 nulls accepts a task on a single lens — **deferred to B with an issue**, because
      `agentTyped` does not change the null rate at `:269` in A.

## Risks & Mitigations

| Risk | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|--------|------------|
| Sketch 1 lands without Sketch 3 | Dead gates silently pass — **measured**: `{"completed":2,"total":2}`, 6 gate invocations, 0 confirmations, phase 2 built on ungated work. Strictly worse than today, where it crashes loudly | Sketches 1+2+3+4 are ONE commit. Guard 7 fails if `:358` reverts; SC-004's floors fail if the gate path is not reached |
| Sketch 3 lands without Sketch 4 | `ReferenceError: ledger is not defined` on every gate failure and every dead gate — the exact paths Sketch 3 fixes. Happy path stays green, so tests that only drive success would miss it | Same one commit. Revision 2 caught the 1→3 coupling and missed this one line away |
| The harness throws a non-Error | `e.message` in the catch throws a TypeError **out of the error handler**, killing the sequential run — the exact US1 scenario. Measured | `e?.message ?? String(e)`, `opts?.label`. SC-001's fixture includes `throw null`. The throw *type* is undocumented — pin it alongside the `parallel()` contract |
| `export const meta` rewrite stops matching | Harness tests a stale shape | SC-006 asserts the rewrite changed the source; a smoke guard asserts it in the suite CI gates on, so it cannot rot where node is absent; `new Function` also throws on an unreplaced `export` |
| Stub `parallel()` drifts from the real contract | Tests prove things about a `parallel` we invented | Run resilience tests against **both** contracts; pin the quoted contract text + date + Claude Code version, re-check at upgrade |
| Harness sloppy vs. module strict | Green CI, ReferenceError in production | `'use strict'` (FR-008), with SC-007's mutation proving it |
| `notAttempted` drifts from reality | Ledger lies about what ran | Derived from the task graph, not tracked separately |

## Deferred — now filed as issues, not stranded in this file

> **All four are filed.** This section is the reasoning; the issues are the record. A backlog that lives
> only in feature A's directory is discoverable only by someone who thinks to look there — which is
> exactly how #27 sat unfiled while `research.md` went stale around it.
>
> - **#27** — `speckit-helper` returns `NO_SPEC` at exit 0 (constitution principle 5 violation; bit this run)
> - **#28** — `:269` two null verdicts shrink the verifier quorum to one lens
> - **#29** — feature B: fan-out amplitude (carries every measured finding below)
> - **#30** — feature C: the single-repo `projectRoot` assumption (carries the four open design questions)

**B (`speckit-workflow-overload`)**: the semaphore *algorithm* survived adversarial attack — no lost
wakeup, no starvation at 200 arrivals, correct under drop-to-1 and nested fan-out, balanced accounting
across the retry path (peak held at exactly 4 with 50 concurrent, a third throwing). Its **argument
validation** did not: `0`/`-1` hang forever spawning nothing; `"abc"` deletes the cap (measured peak
20/20); `2.5` silently becomes 3. `??` does not catch `0` — which is how a user writes "unlimited".
Also: `terminal` must increment on retry-exhaustion, or the throttle is blind to the schema-failure
symptom the field report actually describes. Also: SC-004's "same as v5.0.0" needs a real baseline —
run the harness against `git show v5.0.0:workflows/speckit-workflow.js` and diff the label sequence,
rather than asserting what we remember v5.0.0 did.

**C (`speckit-workflow-multi-repo`)**: the four open cases in the spec's Scope section.

## Quick Start

1. `tests/workflow.test.js` with the Spike 1–3 fixtures — it reproduces the silent drop, the run abort,
   and the null-gate pass as **failing tests** before any fix exists. Spike 2 already proved the first
   two go red; the null-gate test was proven red during review.
2. **Sketches 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 land as ONE commit.** Not 1+2+3 then 4 — that ordering was in revision 2 and
   the adversary proved it ships a `ReferenceError`: Sketch 3's halt body calls `ledger()`, which Sketch 4
   defines. Landing 1+2+3 alone yields a file whose happy path is green and which **throws on every gate
   failure and every dead gate** — precisely the paths Sketch 3 exists to fix. Revision 2 spotted the 1→3
   coupling and missed the 3→4 coupling one line away.
3. Smoke guards, each mutation-tested — including **guard 7 (FR-005)**, which revision 2 promised in its
   Risks table and never wrote.
4. CI step, CHANGELOG, version bump — **all six declarations**, including `README.md` title + footer and
   `.claude/CLAUDE.md` title.
